subsystem: CPython bytes/string escape decoder. The function PyBytes_DecodeEscape takes a raw byte string that still contains C-style backslash escapes (\n, \x41, \007, \\, etc.) and produces a decoded bytes object. It is the routine that materialises the value of a Python bytes literal / repr and, when recode_encoding is set, additionally recodes any non-ASCII source bytes through a target codec. It writes decoded output into a single pre-allocated PyBytes buffer.
provenance: Identified as CPython from the C API surface: Py_ssize_t, PyObject, PyBytes_FromStringAndSize / PyBytes_AsString / PyBytes_AS_STRING / PyBytes_GET_SIZE / _PyBytes_Resize, PyUnicode_DecodeUTF8 / PyUnicode_AsEncodedString, Py_MEMCPY, Py_ISXDIGIT / Py_ISDIGIT / Py_ISLOWER / Py_CHARMASK, PyErr_SetString / PyErr_Format with PyExc_ValueError, and the reference-count macros Py_DECREF. The function name PyBytes_DecodeEscape and the verbatim comment '/* XXX This assumes ASCII! */' place it in Objects/bytesobject.c (the Python 3 descendant of stringobject.c's PyString_DecodeEscape). The sample banner '/* sample: primevul_c_train_2320_vuln */' and the filename eval_0079_cwe190.c are UNTRUSTED metadata and were not relied upon for the analysis.
trust_boundary: The attacker-controlled inputs are the source pointer s and its length len — the escaped byte string being decoded. This data reaches the function when Python parses/evaluates a bytes literal, decodes an escaped string, or is called through any binding that forwards untrusted content and length. The errors and recode_encoding string parameters are also caller-supplied and select behaviour (recode_encoding, when non-NULL, routes non-ASCII runs through an arbitrary target codec). The hostile surface is therefore the byte CONTENT of s, the LENGTH len (which sizes the destination buffer via arithmetic), and the choice of recode_encoding codec (which controls how much a recoded run expands). The output buffer buf is trusted internal memory whose size is derived from len; every write pointer p must stay within it.

what_the_code_does.explanation: The entry point. s is the input byte string containing backslash escapes and len is its length as a signed Py_ssize_t. errors names the error-handling mode ('strict'/'replace'/'ignore') for malformed \x escapes. unicode is passed but not referenced anywhere in this body (a legacy/compat parameter). recode_encoding, when non-NULL, is the name of a target codec through which non-ASCII source bytes are recoded before being emitted. The function returns a new PyObject* bytes on success or NULL on error.

Local state is set up. newlen is the size of the destination buffer: when recode_encoding is NULL the decoded output can never be longer than the input, so newlen = len; when recoding is requested the code pre-multiplies by 4 (newlen = 4*len), a heuristic intended to leave room for a non-ASCII source byte to expand when recoded. PyBytes_FromStringAndSize(NULL, newlen) allocates an uninitialised bytes object of that size; on allocation failure the function returns NULL. p and buf are both set to the writable interior of that object — buf is the fixed start, p is the moving write cursor. end = s + len is the one-past-the-end read sentinel for the input. Crucially, all later writes go through p with NO further bound check against buf+newlen; safety depends entirely on newlen being large enough, which depends on the arithmetic on line 12.

The main scan loop consumes s until it reaches end. If the current byte is not a backslash, control falls to the non_esc label, meaning this byte (or run of bytes) is literal, not an escape sequence. The non_esc label is also a re-entry target used by the default switch case (line 127) to reprocess a byte as literal data.

The recode branch, taken only when recode_encoding is set AND the current byte has its high bit set (a non-ASCII byte). It scans forward with t to cover a maximal run of high-bit bytes (while (t < end && (*t & 0x80)) t++), decodes that run from UTF-8 into a Unicode object u, then re-encodes u into the target codec producing a bytes object w. r/rn are the recoded bytes and their length. Py_MEMCPY(p, r, rn) appends rn recoded bytes into the output at p, then advances p += rn and s = t. This is the only place where a single stretch of input can produce a DIFFERENT (and potentially larger) number of output bytes than input bytes, which is exactly why line 12 pre-scaled the buffer by 4 — but rn is bounded only by whatever the target codec yields, not by 4 per source byte.

The ordinary literal-byte path: a single source byte is copied straight to the output (*p++ = *s++) and the loop continues. In the non-recode case every input byte produces at most one output byte here, which is why newlen = len suffices for that mode.

Reached when *s was a backslash. s is advanced past the backslash; if that lands exactly on end, the string ended with a lone backslash, which is an error ('Trailing \ in string') and jumps to the cleanup label. This is a correct bounds check guarding the subsequent read of the escape character.

The escape dispatch reads the escape character with *s++. A line-continuation '\n' emits nothing. The simple single-character escapes each write exactly one byte. The octal case ('0'-'7') consumes one to three octal digits — each subsequent digit read is guarded by s < end — and writes a single output byte. Every arm here consumes at least the escape character from the input and emits at most one output byte, so in aggregate the escape-decoding path never expands output relative to input consumed.

The \x hex escape. It requires two hex digits and guards the reads with s+1 < end && Py_ISXDIGIT(s[0]) && Py_ISXDIGIT(s[1]) before consuming them, assembling one byte x and writing it (*p++ = x). If the two-digit form is absent, error handling depends on errors: 'strict' (or NULL) raises ValueError with a computed position, 'replace' writes a single '?', 'ignore' writes nothing, and any other code raises. The position argument on line 106, s - 2 - (end - len), computes the index of the offending escape (end - len equals the original start pointer, so s - 2 - start is the byte offset), but it is a pointer difference (ptrdiff_t/Py_ssize_t) passed to a %d conversion. Each arm here writes at most one output byte.

The default case handles an unrecognised escape (e.g. '\z'): the backslash is preserved literally (*p++ = '\\'), s is rewound by one so the following character is not lost, and control jumps back to non_esc to reprocess that character as literal/recodable data. The comment notes that an arbitrary run of unescaped UTF-8 bytes may follow, which is precisely how the recode branch at line 21 gets fed a long high-bit run.

After the scan, if the actual output length p-buf is smaller than the allocated newlen (the common case, since escapes shrink and the 4x recode estimate is usually an over-allocation), the bytes object is shrunk to the exact size with _PyBytes_Resize. The result is returned. The failed label drops the reference to v and returns NULL. Note this final check only shrinks the buffer; it does NOT and cannot retroactively catch a write that already ran past buf+newlen during the loop.

what_could_go_wrong.code:
Py_ssize_t newlen = recode_encoding ? 4*len:len;
    v = PyBytes_FromStringAndSize((char *)NULL, newlen);
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: Integer overflow in the buffer-size computation. When recode_encoding is non-NULL the destination size is newlen = 4*len. len is a signed Py_ssize_t carrying the attacker-controlled input length; the multiplication 4*len is performed in Py_ssize_t and is NOT checked for overflow. On a 64-bit build, an input longer than PY_SSIZE_T_MAX/4 (roughly 2^61 bytes), or on a 32-bit build an input longer than ~536 MB (PY_SSIZE_T_MAX/4 == ~0x1FFFFFFF), makes 4*len wrap around: it becomes a small positive value or a negative value. PyBytes_FromStringAndSize then allocates a buffer far smaller than len (or fails/aborts on a negative), yet the scan loop proceeds to write up to len-plus-expansion bytes through p with no per-write bound check. The result is a controlled heap buffer overflow with attacker-controlled content — a memory-corruption / possible-RCE primitive. The invariant the implementation must uphold: compute the buffer size with overflow-checked arithmetic (reject or clamp when 4*len would overflow Py_ssize_t / PY_SSIZE_T_MAX) BEFORE allocating. This is the labelled defect (CWE-190) and matches the known CPython escape-decoder integer-overflow heap overflow.

Out-of-bounds write from an unchecked recode expansion, independent of the len overflow. Py_MEMCPY(p, r, rn) appends rn bytes into the output buffer with NO check that p + rn stays within buf + newlen. rn is PyBytes_GET_SIZE(w), the length produced by re-encoding a non-ASCII run through the attacker-selectable recode_encoding codec. The 4*len pre-scaling assumes a source byte expands to at most 4 output bytes, but that assumption is codec-specific and not enforced here: a target encoding that expands a run to more than 4 bytes per consumed source byte (for example multibyte/stateful codecs, or an encoding that emits a byte-order mark / escape sequences) makes the cumulative rn exceed newlen and overflows the heap buffer. Attacker controls the high-bit byte content and, in some call paths, the codec name. The invariant: bound each Py_MEMCPY against the remaining buffer (buf + newlen - p) and grow the buffer if needed, rather than trusting a fixed 4x estimate. This is a latent OOB write (CWE-787) living in the same output-sizing contract as line 12.

Format-specifier / integer-truncation hygiene issue. The position argument s - 2 - (end - len) is a pointer difference whose type is ptrdiff_t / Py_ssize_t (64-bit on LP64 platforms), but it is consumed by a %d conversion, which expects a 32-bit int. For inputs longer than INT_MAX, or simply on any 64-bit platform where the va_arg width mismatches, this produces a wrong/truncated position in the error message and is technically undefined behaviour for the varargs call. Impact is low — it is a diagnostic string, not a memory write — but it is a real correctness/robustness defect and worth noting (CWE-704/CWE-190-adjacent truncation). The invariant: format Py_ssize_t values with the %zd length modifier.

This trailing resize is defensive only in the shrinking direction and gives a false sense of safety: it runs AFTER the loop, so any overflow from line 12 or line 42 has already corrupted the heap before this point. It never detects or repairs an over-write. Reviewers should not read this line as a bounds guard — the only real guards must be applied at allocation time (line 12) and at each recode append (line 42).

summary: This is CPython's PyBytes_DecodeEscape byte-escape decoder. Its central defect is an unchecked signed multiplication 4*len (line 12) that sizes the output buffer when recode_encoding is set: a sufficiently long attacker-controlled input overflows Py_ssize_t, under-allocates the buffer, and the loop then writes past it — a heap buffer overflow with controlled content (CWE-190 leading to CWE-787), corresponding to the known CPython escape-decoder integer-overflow CVE. A second, related latent overflow exists at the recode Py_MEMCPY (line 42), which appends codec output with no bound check against the buffer, trusting a fixed 4x expansion estimate.

reviewed_anchors.reason: s/len are the attacker-controlled input; len feeds the overflowing buffer-size arithmetic.
Not referenced anywhere in the function body; carries no active contract here.
Selects the 4*len buffer branch and the recode path; its codec choice governs output expansion.
Only selects strict/replace/ignore error behaviour for \x; each arm writes at most one byte, no size contract.
Unchecked signed multiplication 4*len overflows and under-sizes the buffer — the core CWE-190.
Allocates the sole output buffer from the overflowed newlen; the wrong size propagates from here.
Write cursor/base; safe as long as newlen is correct — captured by the line-12 finding.
Read sentinel; all reads are correctly guarded with s < end / s+1 < end throughout the switch.
Correct gate; the risk is not the gate but the unbounded append it leads to (line 42).
Run scan is correctly bounded by end; no over-read.
Appends codec output with no check against buf+newlen; overflows if expansion exceeds the assumed 4x.
One-in/one-out literal copy; bounded by newlen=len in non-recode mode.
Correct guard preventing an over-read of the escape character.
Each digit read guarded by s < end; writes exactly one output byte — no expansion or over-read.
Reads guarded by s+1 < end / s < end; writes at most one byte per escape — no memory-safety contract violated.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: PyErr_Format position arg s - 2 - (end - len) with %d (lines 104-106)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Py_ssize_t/ptrdiff passed to %d — truncation/varargs mismatch; low severity diagnostic bug.
Rewind+reprocess is correct; the s-- is compensated and cannot underflow past the just-read escape.
Post-loop shrink only; not a bounds guard, and cannot repair a prior overflow — noted in walkthrough.

findings.title: Integer overflow in 4*len output-buffer sizing leads to heap buffer overflow
findings.bug_class: Integer overflow in size calculation leading to heap buffer overflow (out-of-bounds write)
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/eval/eval_0079_cwe190.c (this file; buffer written by the loop at lines 42/47/60-78/100/110)
findings.invariant: The output buffer size must be computed with overflow-checked arithmetic: reject or clamp before allocation when 4*len would exceed PY_SSIZE_T_MAX, so newlen always >= the maximum number of bytes the loop can write.
findings.failure_mode: With recode_encoding non-NULL and an attacker-supplied len greater than PY_SSIZE_T_MAX/4 (~0x1FFFFFFF bytes on 32-bit, ~2^61 on 64-bit), the signed multiplication 4*len wraps to a small or negative Py_ssize_t. PyBytes_FromStringAndSize allocates a buffer far smaller than the input, and the scan loop then writes up to len (plus recode expansion) bytes through p with no per-write bound check, corrupting the heap with attacker-controlled data — potential arbitrary code execution.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm there is no overflow guard between line 12 and line 13, that len is directly caller/attacker-controlled on the reachable path, and that PyBytes_FromStringAndSize does not itself reject the wrapped size before the loop writes.

findings.title: Unbounded recode append (Py_MEMCPY) overflows the output buffer when codec expansion exceeds 4x
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds write (buffer sizing assumption / missing bounds check)
findings.invariant: Every append into the output must be bounded against the remaining space buf + newlen - p, and the buffer grown when insufficient; the fixed 4-bytes-per-source-byte estimate on line 12 must be a proven upper bound for the chosen recode_encoding, or enforced dynamically.
findings.failure_mode: For a non-ASCII run, rn = PyBytes_GET_SIZE(w) is the length produced by re-encoding through the attacker-influenced recode_encoding codec. The code copies rn bytes to p and advances p with no comparison to buf+newlen. A codec whose output exceeds 4 bytes per consumed source byte (multibyte/stateful encodings, or output carrying a BOM/escape sequences) makes cumulative writes pass the end of the buffer, an out-of-bounds heap write with attacker-controlled content — even when 4*len does not integer-overflow.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm that no reachable recode_encoding value can expand a byte run beyond 4x, and that there is no bounds check or buffer growth guarding the Py_MEMCPY on line 42.

findings.title: Py_ssize_t position passed to %d in error message (truncation / varargs mismatch)
findings.invariant: A Py_ssize_t/ptrdiff_t argument must be formatted with the matching length modifier (%zd), not %d, so the reported position is correct and the varargs read is well-defined on all platforms.
findings.failure_mode: s - 2 - (end - len) is a pointer difference of Py_ssize_t width (64-bit on LP64) but is consumed by a 32-bit %d conversion. For inputs where the offset exceeds INT_MAX, or on any platform where the argument width differs from int, the reported position is truncated/garbage and the varargs call is technically undefined. Impact is limited to a misleading diagnostic — no memory corruption — but it is a real robustness defect.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the format string uses %d against a Py_ssize_t argument and that inputs large enough to exceed INT_MAX can reach this branch.

audit_checklist: Verify 4*len on line 12 is overflow-checked (compared against PY_SSIZE_T_MAX/4) BEFORE PyBytes_FromStringAndSize on line 13, on every path where recode_encoding is non-NULL.
Trace how len reaches this function and whether an attacker can drive it above PY_SSIZE_T_MAX/4 (32-bit builds are reachable at ~536 MB inputs).
Confirm the recode Py_MEMCPY on line 42 is bounded against buf+newlen (or the buffer is grown), and prove the maximum expansion of every reachable recode_encoding codec is <= 4 bytes per source byte.
Check whether PyBytes_FromStringAndSize rejects negative/zero sizes and whether the loop can still write when newlen wrapped to a small positive value.
Confirm _PyBytes_Resize on line 132 is understood as shrink-only and provides no protection against the loop over-writing.
Replace %d with %zd for the Py_ssize_t position argument on line 106.
Re-derive that in the non-recode path (newlen == len) every switch arm writes at most one output byte per source byte consumed, so no overflow is possible there.
